blocks.text: Chapter 4.
The Buddy System: Building a Truthseeking Group
The ideas in 30 seconds

Solo debiasing mostly fails because reasoning evolved to win arguments, not to find truth; the same confirmation bias that traps an individual becomes an asset when a group evaluates competing arguments.
Groups only improve thinking if they are chartered for accuracy — unstructured groups of like-minded people drift toward more extreme versions of their shared beliefs (Sunstein's group polarization).
Erik Seidel's elite poker circle models the fix: ban bad-beat stories and complaints about luck, and reserve group time for strategy questions where the answer could actually change.

Robert Merton's CUDOS norms — Communism (share all data), Universalism (judge the message, not the messenger), Disinterestedness (strip out conflicts of interest, especially outcome knowledge), and Organized Skepticism (make dissent the default) — give any truthseeking group its operating rules.
Once you join such a group, accountability works even in its absence: anticipating that you will have to justify a decision to the pod changes how you decide in the moment, alone.
Why You Can't Debias Yourself Alone

Knowing about motivated reasoning, self-serving bias, and blind-spot bias does not neutralize them — smarter people are often better at rationalizing, not worse. The biases run on fast, automatic processing, and willpower-based vigilance is exactly the kind of slow, effortful monitoring we abandon the moment we're tired, threatened, or invested in being right.

Cognitive scientists Hugo Mercier and Dan Sperber offer an explanation in their argumentative theory of reasoning: human reason did not evolve as a private truth-finding instrument. It evolved socially, to construct persuasive arguments and to evaluate the arguments of others. Seen this way, confirmation bias isn't a design flaw — it's the engine of advocacy. An individual reasoning alone is a lawyer building one side's case.

The same architecture becomes a strength in a group. Mercier and Sperber found that while individuals are lazy and biased when generating arguments, people are demanding and fairly objective when evaluating someone else's arguments. Put several motivated advocates in a room with a norm of mutual evaluation and the biases start canceling instead of compounding. My blind spots are invisible to me but obvious to you, and vice versa — blind-spot bias, the tendency to see bias in others but not ourselves, is precisely why outsourcing the error-checking works.

Duke's own development as a player followed this path. Her brother Howard and his circle of world-class players didn't hand her sympathy after losses; they interrogated her decisions. The group did the debiasing she could not do for herself, converting the raw experience of wins and losses into calibrated lessons. The practical conclusion: you don't need to become an unbiased person — an impossible project — you need to join or build a small pod (even two or three people suffices) whose job is to catch what you can't.
Not All Groups Make You Smarter

A group is an amplifier, and it amplifies whatever it rewards. Legal scholar Cass Sunstein documented group polarization: when like-minded people deliberate together, they don't moderate — they exit the discussion holding more extreme versions of the views they walked in with. Echo chambers aren't neutral; they are bias-multiplication machines. A pod that rewards commiseration will make you a more confident complainer, not a better decision-maker.

Duke illustrates the wrong kind of group with poker's ubiquitous shared ritual: swapping stories about unfairness. Most players' social circles function as mutual validation societies — everyone confirms everyone else's terrible luck, and no one's play improves. The default social contract in most groups, in poker and in offices, is "I'll agree with your version if you agree with mine."

Because truthseeking violates that default contract, it has to be opted into explicitly. Duke's example is a David Letterman interview with actress Lauren Graham: when Graham described the drama in her life, Letterman skipped sympathy and suggested that maybe she was the common element — perhaps she just had "crazy" friends by choice. It landed badly, not because the hypothesis was worthless but because Graham hadn't signed up for truthseeking; she'd come for a sympathetic chat. The lesson cuts both ways: don't impose accuracy-first feedback on people who haven't agreed to it (Chapter 5 covers how to talk with non-truthseekers), and within your pod, make the agreement explicit — a group charter stating that the goal is accuracy, that dissent is welcome, and that members will point out each other's motivated reasoning without it being taken as an attack.

A good pod also needs diversity of viewpoint. A group that shares all your priors can execute the charter perfectly and still polarize, because nobody in the room can generate the disconfirming argument.
Erik Seidel's Rule: No Bad-Beat Stories

Erik Seidel — a poker player with a decades-long record at the very top of the game — enforces a simple filter in his circle: he refuses to listen to bad-beat stories, the poker term for tales of how an unlucky card cost you a pot you deserved. Complaints about luck are conversationally satisfying and informationally empty. Luck is the part of the outcome you couldn't control; there is nothing to learn from re-narrating it, and rehearsing it trains self-serving bias — the Chapter 3 habit of shoveling every loss into the luck bucket.

Seidel's circle reserves its attention for a different kind of exchange: "I faced this situation, here were the considerations, what would you have done and why?" Strategy questions have answers that can change future behavior; luck stories don't. The rule effectively redirects the group's scarcest resources — attention and approval — toward decision analysis.

This is where the group plugs into the habit machinery from Chapter 3. We repeat what gets rewarded. If your social reward comes from being pitied, you'll produce grievances; if it comes from offering a well-reasoned hand analysis or admitting an error the group helps you find, you'll produce those instead. A truthseeking pod deliberately re-engineers the reward: approval flows to accuracy, to updating, to saying "I think I played that badly," rather than to victimhood or bragging. Over time, members start wanting to find their own mistakes, because mistake-finding is the currency of status inside the group.

The filter also protects the group's time. A pod that tolerates venting becomes a support group; a pod that bans it stays a learning group. Support has its place — but be honest about which group you're in, and keep at least one that is unambiguously about getting better.
CUDOS: The Operating Rules of a Truthseeking Pod

For the norms themselves, Duke borrows from sociologist Robert Merton, who in 1942 codified the ethos that makes science self-correcting. His acronym CUDOS names four norms, and each maps directly onto group decision hygiene.

Communism (data-sharing, not politics): within the group, information is common property. Don't curate the story of your decision to look good — share all the details, including the ones that incriminate you. Duke notes that people naturally tell "groomed" versions of events, omitting the fact that undermines their preferred narrative; a truthseeking group treats withheld or trimmed data as a violation, and members learn to ask, "What are you leaving out?" More detail means better diagnosis; a doctor can't work from a censored chart.

Universalism: evaluate the claim independently of who makes it. The messenger's status, likability, or track record is not evidence about the message. Duke admits she long dismissed strategic observations from players she'd labeled as bad — and lost learning opportunities every time. The discipline works in both directions: don't reject ideas from low-status sources, and don't wave through ideas from high-status ones. A useful exercise: consider the argument as if you didn't know who offered it.

Disinterestedness: keep conflicts of interest out of the analysis. The most insidious conflict is knowing how things turned out, because outcome knowledge triggers hindsight bias and resulting (Chapter 1) in the listeners. The fix is outcome-blind analysis: when presenting a decision for review, stop the story before the result — "I got my chips in with a pair against a flush draw; thoughts?" — and let the group judge the decision on the information available at the time. Duke recounts telling hand histories this way and watching the quality of feedback jump; once people hear the ending, they reason backward from it.

Organized Skepticism: make doubt a communal duty rather than an insult. In a CUDOS group, skepticism is how you engage with a claim, not how you dismiss a person — expressed as questions ("what would make this wrong?") rather than verdicts. Dissent isn't disloyalty; it's the membership fee.
Accountability That Works When the Group Isn't There

The final payoff of a truthseeking pod is that it changes your thinking even in its absence. Accountability — knowing you will have to explain your decision to people who will judge the reasoning, not the result — recruits your future embarrassment as a present-tense decision aid.

Duke describes the mechanism from the table: mid-hand, steaming after a loss and tempted by a reckless call, she would hear the question her group would ask — "why did you do that?" — and fold. The anticipated conversation functioned as a real-time filter. Psychologically, this pre-loads deliberative System 2 thinking into moments that reflexive System 1 would otherwise own, because you are effectively never deciding alone; the imagined audience is always present. (Chapter 5 extends this with Tetlock's research on accountability to audiences with unknown views, and Chapter 6 adds tools for defeating your in-the-moment self.)

Accountability also reshapes what you do after decisions. Knowing the pod will demand the full, ungroomed account (Communism) and will hear it outcome-blind (Disinterestedness), you start recording decisions honestly as you make them — you can't retro-fit a flattering story you'll be cross-examined on. And because the group awards credit for finding your own errors, the ledger you keep tilts toward truth.

Duke's summary image is borrowed from John Stuart Mill, whom she quotes on why exposure to the other side is non-negotiable: "He who knows only his own side of the case, knows little of that." A good pod guarantees you never know only your own side. Three people, a charter, CUDOS norms, and a ban on bad-beat stories — that is the entire technology, and it compounds every week you run it.
In practice

Recruit one to three people this week for an explicit decision pod; agree in writing on the charter: accuracy over affirmation, dissent welcome, no penalty for finding each other's motivated reasoning.
Ban bad-beat stories in that group — no venting about luck, bosses, or markets; convert every complaint into a decision question ("given what I knew, what should I have done?").
Present decisions for review outcome-blind: describe the situation and your reasoning, stop before the result, and collect the group's read before revealing how it turned out.
Share the full, ungroomed record — including the details that make you look worse — and ask reviewers "what am I leaving out?" to enforce it.
Evaluate the next idea you receive as if you didn't know its source; deliberately mine one insight from someone you're inclined to dismiss.
Before your next consequential solo decision, ask aloud how your group would cross-examine it — and don't proceed until you could survive the questioning.
